content.text: AWS provides two complementary tools for monitoring service health: the public Service Health Dashboard at status.aws.amazon.com (visible to anyone) and AWS Health — a personalized view inside the AWS Console that surfaces events specifically affecting your account's resources. Understanding both, and knowing how to automate responses to health events, is a core operational skill.

attrs.alt: Architecture diagram comparing the AWS Service Health Dashboard (public, global view) with AWS Health (personal, account-specific), plus the EventBridge automation flow

content.text: shows real-time status for every AWS service in every region. It requires no AWS account and serves two audiences: the public checking on an outage in progress, and engineers who want a historical view of past incidents.
— Operational (green), Service disruption (orange), Service outage (red). Each changes as AWS engineers update the incident.
— shows service-level status per region; it does NOT show account-specific impact. An outage affecting 0.1% of EC2 instances may show as orange, but your instances may be unaffected.
— each service+region combination has its own feed URL. You can ingest these feeds into a monitoring system to auto-create incidents.
— incidents from the past are listed with timestamps and update history; useful for post-mortems and SLA evidence.
— updates can lag; AWS engineers must manually publish updates while simultaneously fighting the outage. Cross-reference with the AWS Health API for account-specific reality.

AWS Health (formerly Personal Health Dashboard) provides a personalized view of events that affect your specific AWS resources. It goes far beyond the public dashboard: if an EC2 host in your account is scheduled for retirement, or if a region's SQS service is degraded for a subset of customers that includes your account, AWS Health surfaces that event while the public dashboard may still show green.
— an active service disruption affecting your account right now. Contains affected resource IDs where known.
— planned maintenance AWS will perform on your resources (host retirement, hardware refresh, SSL certificate rotation). Requires action within the given window.
— informational notices (root MFA enforcement deadlines, region enablement, policy changes).
— each event may list specific resource ARNs or instance IDs that are affected, letting you scope impact precisely.
The AWS Health API lets you programmatically query events and affected entities. It requires
AWS Business Support or AWS Enterprise Support
— it is not available on the Basic or Developer Support plans. This is a critical operational distinction.
The Health API endpoint is global (always
) but returns events for all regions. Querying any other regional endpoint raises an error.
The most powerful way to use AWS Health is through EventBridge. Every health event is automatically published to EventBridge under the source
. You can write rules to match specific event types and trigger Lambda functions, SNS notifications, or Step Functions workflows.

The matching Lambda receives the full event payload including the list of affected EC2 instance IDs, letting it automatically trigger failover, replacement, or a PagerDuty alert before the maintenance window begins.

def handler(event, context):
    detail = event['detail']
    affected = detail.get('affectedEntities', [])
    event_code = detail['eventTypeCode']

    instance_ids = [e['entityValue'] for e in affected]

    if event_code == 'AWS_EC2_INSTANCE_RETIREMENT_SCHEDULED':
        print(f"Instances retiring: {instance_ids}")
        # Trigger ASG instance refresh, stop+start, or replacement
        ec2 = boto3.client('ec2')
        ec2.stop_instances(InstanceIds=instance_ids)
        # Replacement is handled by Auto Scaling Group on next health check
A complete Terraform module wires AWS Health events to SNS alerts in under 30 lines:
EC2 Instance Retirement: A Worked Example

EC2 instance retirement is the most common scheduled health event. AWS detects hardware degradation and marks the underlying physical host for replacement. Your instance receives a retirement date; if you take no action, AWS stops (for EBS-backed) or terminates (for instance-store) the instance on that date.
health event listing your instance ID and the retirement date.
: stop and start the instance. AWS migrates it to healthy hardware; the public IP changes but the Elastic IP and EBS volumes remain.
instances in an Auto Scaling Group
: terminate the instance. The ASG immediately replaces it on healthy hardware.
: termination destroys local storage. Ensure data is persisted to EBS or S3 before the retirement date.

# Query retirement events via CLI (requires Business/Enterprise Support)
aws health describe-events \
  --filter eventTypeCategories=scheduledChange,eventTypeCode=AWS_EC2_INSTANCE_RETIREMENT_SCHEDULED \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'events[*].{Code:eventTypeCode,Region:region,Status:statusCode}' \
  --output table

In a multi-account AWS Organizations setup, you can enable the Organizational View feature in AWS Health. This aggregates health events across all member accounts into the management account — critical for cloud operations teams managing hundreds of accounts.
from the management account. The Health service-linked role is created automatically in each member account.
— health events from member accounts propagate to the management account's default event bus, enabling centralized alerting.

# List events affecting any account in the org (run from management account)
aws health describe-events-for-organization \
  --filter eventTypeCategories=issue \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'events[*].{Service:service,Region:region,Status:statusCode}'
EC2, RDS, S3, ECS, IAM, etc.
ISO 8601 timestamps; endTime absent while event is open
Present for AZ-scoped events; absent for region-wide events

There are two modes of using AWS Health: reactive (looking things up after something breaks) and proactive (automating responses before they impact users). Proactive use requires EventBridge automation or Trusted Advisor checks.
Confirm whether AWS or your code is at fault
Ops team knows before users complain
Retirement handled with zero human action
Single pane of glass across 100+ accounts
Public dashboard shows green, users report errors
— the public dashboard reflects broad service health. Your account may be in an affected subset. Always check AWS Health (personal) and CloudWatch metrics for your specific resources.
Health API unavailable on Basic Support
if you call it without Business/Enterprise Support. Budget for this tier in production environments.
— health events are visible in the console and API but are NOT sent via email by default. You must wire up EventBridge → SNS to receive email notifications.
even for resources in other regions. Calling it against
silently returns empty results on some SDK versions.
— in multi-account setups, each member account's events are invisible from the management account unless you explicitly enable Organizational View. A single API call fixes this.

AWS Trusted Advisor complements Health by checking your account configuration against AWS best practices across five categories: cost optimization, performance, security, fault tolerance, and service limits. While Health surfaces events (things happening to your resources), Trusted Advisor surfaces risks (configurations that could lead to future problems).
Current and historical AWS service status
Confirming if AWS is having an outage
Proactive risk reduction before an event

Trusted Advisor checks that often predict Health events include: EC2 instances with low instance-store backup protection, EBS volumes without snapshots, and service limit utilization above 80% (approaching the hard ceiling which can trigger throttling events surfaced in Health).
When your usage approaches a service quota (formerly called service limit), AWS may send an
health event warning you. If usage hits the hard limit, requests start failing — which can trigger a secondary
— request increases proactively before hitting limits. Integrated with Trusted Advisor's Service Limits check.
namespace publishes metric for many quotas (e.g.,
). Alarm at 80% to catch limits before they bite.
— the Service Quotas API allows automated quota increase requests; combine with a Lambda triggered by a CloudWatch alarm.

import boto3

sq = boto3.client('service-quotas')

# List current quotas for EC2
quotas = sq.list_service_quotas(ServiceCode='ec2')
for q in quotas['Quotas']:
    print(q['QuotaName'], q['Value'], q['Adjustable'])

# Request an increase
sq.request_service_quota_increase(
    ServiceCode='ec2',
    QuotaCode='L-1216C47A',   # Running On-Demand Standard Instances
    DesiredValue=500.0
)
Using Health Data in Incident Management
During an incident, the first question is always: is this us, or is this AWS? A structured incident checklist prevents wasted debugging time when AWS is at fault.
— open console → AWS Health → Dashboard. Look for open events in the impacted region and service.
— if the public dashboard is red for the same service, you are confirmed as affected by an AWS-side event. Stop debugging application code.
— expand the event to see if your specific resource ARNs or instance IDs appear. A region-wide event may not affect your AZ.
Set a health event update cadence
— AWS updates health events as the incident evolves. Refresh every 15–30 minutes and track the update history.
— use the closed event's description and timestamps as evidence in your own post-mortem and for SLA credit claims with customers.
Most teams want health events in Slack or PagerDuty alongside their application alerts. The canonical pattern is: EventBridge → SNS → Lambda → webhook.

import json, urllib.request, os

def handler(event, context):
    detail = event['detail']
    service = detail['service']
    event_type = detail['eventTypeCode']
    region = detail['eventRegion']
    status = detail['statusCode']
    description = detail.get('eventDescription', [{}])[0].get('latestDescription', '')

    text = (
        f":warning: *AWS Health Event*\n"
        f"Service: {service} | Region: {region} | Status: {status}\n"
        f"Type: `{event_type}`\n"
        f"Details: {description[:200]}"
    )

    payload = json.dumps({"text": text}).encode()
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        os.environ['SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL'],
        data=payload,
        headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json'}
    )
    urllib.request.urlopen(req)

Store the Slack webhook URL in AWS Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store (SecureString). Pass it to the Lambda via an environment variable resolved from the parameter at deploy time, not hardcoded in source.

AWS sends scheduledChange events days or weeks before planned maintenance. The most common examples are RDS minor version upgrades, ElastiCache engine upgrades, and EC2 host maintenance. Ignoring these means AWS proceeds at the announced time, which may coincide with peak traffic.

— set a preferred maintenance window during low-traffic hours. AWS reschedules within your window. If you enable auto minor version upgrade, AWS applies patches automatically; otherwise it waits for your manual action.
— you can initiate the maintenance early via the console or CLI:
. Useful if you want to control the exact timing.

— Aurora Multi-AZ with read replicas performs failover automatically during maintenance; the primary writer flips to a replica with minimal downtime (~30 s). Aurora serverless v2 also handles this transparently.
— Redis cluster-mode enabled clusters perform rolling upgrades node by node. For cluster-mode disabled, there is a brief primary failover.

# Apply RDS pending maintenance now (rather than waiting for the window)
aws rds apply-pending-maintenance-action \
  --resource-identifier arn:aws:rds:us-east-1:123456789012:db:prod-db \
  --apply-action system-update \
  --opt-in-type immediate

# Check pending maintenance on all RDS instances
aws rds describe-pending-maintenance-actions \
  --query 'PendingMaintenanceActions[*].{Resource:ResourceIdentifier,Action:PendingMaintenanceActionDetails[0].Action,ApplyDate:PendingMaintenanceActionDetails[0].AutoAppliedAfterDate}'

Knowing that your automation will fire correctly when a real Health event arrives requires testing. AWS Fault Injection Service (FIS) can simulate AWS service disruptions so you can validate your EventBridge rules and Lambda handlers before a real event occurs.
For scheduledChange events that cannot be injected via FIS, manually publish a synthetic EventBridge event to test your Lambda handler end-to-end:
Teams that skip Health automation often discover the gap at the worst moment. Three common failure modes:
— AWS stops the instance on the retirement date. If the instance was not in an ASG, it comes back stopped. The application is down until someone manually starts it — possibly hours later.
— the maintenance window fires at 03:00 UTC Sunday and causes a 60-second failover. Engineers find the slow-query log has changed format (minor version bump) and a monitoring query breaks.
— AWS sends an accountNotification that a root API key has been detected in a public repository. Without Health alerting, the notice sits unread in the console for days while the key is exploited.
Configure EventBridge → SNS for ALL health events as a day-one baseline, even if the Lambda automation comes later. Getting an email about a retirement event beats finding out from users.
List open issues in your account
Get affected resources for an event
All Health API calls must be made in the
region regardless of where the affected resources reside. Add
explicitly to avoid the default region returning an endpoint error.
The public Service Health Dashboard shows global status; AWS Health shows account-specific events that may not appear on the public page.
Health events come in three categories: issue (active), scheduledChange (maintenance), and accountNotification (informational).
The AWS Health API requires Business or Enterprise Support; wire it to EventBridge for automated alerting and remediation.
EC2 instance retirement is handled by stopping and starting (EBS-backed) or by terminating and letting an ASG replace the instance.
Enable Organizational View in a multi-account Organizations setup to aggregate health events from all member accounts into a single management account.
